title: AI Governance Evidence Items
description: Evidence items that demonstrate compliance with each control. Organized by control with evidence type classification.

evidenceTypes.record: Logs, test results, audit trails, and operational records
evidenceTypes.artifact: Technical outputs, configurations, and system documentation

items.name: AI Governance Charter
items.description: Defines AI governance body, authority, scope, and reporting structure.
whatGoodLooksLike: Charter clearly defines governance body mandate, authority boundaries, and escalation paths
Signed by board or executive sponsor with explicit budget and resource allocation
Reviewed and updated at least annually with version history maintained
commonGaps: Charter exists but lacks defined authority or decision-making power
No review cadence or version control — document is stale
Missing link between governance body and operational AI teams

items.name: AI RACI Matrix
items.description: Responsibility assignment matrix for AI lifecycle activities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Covers all AI lifecycle phases from design through decommissioning
Names specific roles (not just departments) with clear accountable owners
Aligned with organizational chart and updated when roles change
commonGaps: RACI exists for development only, missing monitoring and incident response phases
Uses generic department names instead of named accountable individuals
Not updated after organizational restructuring

items.name: Governance Meeting Minutes
items.description: Minutes from AI governance committee meetings showing oversight decisions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes document attendees, agenda items, decisions made, and action items with owners
Meeting frequency matches the cadence defined in the governance charter
Action items are tracked to completion with status updates in subsequent meetings
commonGaps: Meetings occur but minutes are not formally recorded or retained
Minutes lack decision rationale — only record outcomes without discussion context
No evidence of follow-up on action items from prior meetings

items.name: Annual Governance Review Report
items.description: Annual review of governance framework effectiveness.
whatGoodLooksLike: Report assesses governance framework effectiveness against defined KPIs
Includes recommendations for improvement with implementation timeline
Benchmarks against industry standards or regulatory expectations
commonGaps: Review is a checkbox exercise with no substantive assessment of effectiveness
No metrics or KPIs defined to measure governance performance
Recommendations from prior reviews not addressed or tracked

items.name: AI Risk Assessment Template
items.description: Standardized template for conducting AI system risk assessments.
whatGoodLooksLike: Template covers technical, ethical, legal, and societal risk dimensions
Includes scoring methodology with defined likelihood and impact scales
Aligned with EU AI Act risk categories and organizational risk appetite
commonGaps: Template focuses only on technical risks, missing ethical and societal dimensions
No scoring methodology — risks described qualitatively without prioritization
Not aligned with regulatory risk classification requirements

items.name: Completed Risk Assessments
items.description: Filled risk assessments for each deployed AI system.
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk assessment completed for every deployed AI system with no gaps in coverage
Each assessment identifies specific mitigations mapped to controls
Assessments are reviewed and updated at defined intervals or after significant changes
commonGaps: Only high-profile AI systems assessed — shadow AI or low-risk systems skipped
Assessments completed at deployment but never revisited or updated
Mitigations identified but not mapped to specific controls or owners

items.name: Risk Register
items.description: Centralized register of identified AI risks with status and mitigation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Single centralized register covering all AI systems with consistent risk taxonomy
Each risk has an assigned owner, mitigation status, and review date
Register is actively maintained with risks added, updated, and closed over time
commonGaps: Multiple disconnected risk lists across teams with no central view
Risks added but never updated or closed — register grows stale
No ownership assigned to individual risks

items.name: Risk Classification Decision Log
items.description: Documentation of risk tier classification decisions with rationale.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each AI system has a documented risk tier with clear rationale referencing classification criteria
Classification decisions are reviewed and approved by appropriate authority
Reclassification triggers are defined and acted upon when system scope changes
commonGaps: Systems classified informally without documented rationale
No reclassification process when AI system scope or context changes
Classification criteria not aligned with EU AI Act risk tiers

items.name: Data Governance Policy
items.description: Policy governing collection, use, storage, and disposal of AI training data.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy addresses data quality criteria specific to AI training (representativeness, completeness, accuracy)
Defines roles and responsibilities for data stewardship of AI datasets
Includes data retention and disposal requirements aligned with legal obligations
commonGaps: Generic data governance policy with no AI-specific provisions
No data quality criteria defined for training data
Missing data provenance and lineage requirements

items.name: Data Provenance Records
items.description: Lineage documentation showing data sources, transformations, and quality checks.
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete lineage from original source through all transformations to model input
Each dataset has documented legal basis for collection and processing
Provenance records are versioned and linked to specific model training runs
commonGaps: Data sources documented at high level but transformations not tracked
No legal basis documented for data collection or processing
Provenance not linked to specific model versions or training runs

items.name: Data Quality Reports
items.description: Automated data quality assessment results (completeness, accuracy, representativeness).
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports generated automatically as part of data pipeline with defined quality thresholds
Metrics cover completeness, accuracy, consistency, and representativeness across demographics
Quality failures trigger alerts and block model training until resolved
commonGaps: Quality checks are manual and sporadic rather than automated
Metrics focus on completeness only, missing representativeness and bias indicators
No defined thresholds — quality assessed subjectively

items.name: Data Processing Impact Assessment
items.description: DPIA or equivalent for personal data used in AI training.
whatGoodLooksLike: DPIA completed before personal data is used for AI training
Assessment identifies specific privacy risks and documents mitigations
Reviewed by data protection officer or equivalent authority
commonGaps: No DPIA conducted for AI training data containing personal information
DPIA completed as formality without substantive risk analysis
No DPO review or sign-off on the assessment

items.name: Bias Testing Methodology
items.description: Documented methodology for fairness testing across protected characteristics.
whatGoodLooksLike: Methodology defines specific fairness metrics appropriate to the use case (e.g., demographic parity, equalized odds)
Protected characteristics to test are explicitly enumerated with justification
Methodology specifies testing frequency, sample size requirements, and acceptance thresholds
commonGaps: No documented methodology — bias testing performed ad hoc
Fairness metrics chosen without consideration of use case context
Protected characteristics not explicitly defined or incomplete

items.name: Bias Test Results
items.description: Results of bias testing including fairness metrics and identified disparities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Results report fairness metrics across all defined protected characteristics
Disparities are quantified with statistical significance and compared to thresholds
Results are linked to specific model versions and test datasets
commonGaps: Testing performed but results not formally documented or retained
Only aggregate metrics reported — no breakdown by protected group
No comparison against defined acceptance thresholds

items.name: Bias Mitigation Actions
items.description: Documented corrective actions taken to address identified bias.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each identified disparity has a documented mitigation action with owner and deadline
Mitigation effectiveness is measured with before/after fairness metrics
Actions include both technical fixes and process improvements
commonGaps: Bias identified but no corrective actions documented or taken
Mitigations applied without measuring effectiveness
Only technical debiasing attempted — no process or data collection changes considered

items.name: Diverse Review Panel Records
items.description: Composition and findings of diverse panels reviewing AI for bias.
whatGoodLooksLike: Panel composition documented with demographic diversity and domain expertise represented
Panel findings are formally recorded with specific recommendations
Panel reviews occur at defined lifecycle stages (design, pre-deployment, post-deployment)
commonGaps: No diverse review panel established
Panel exists but composition lacks meaningful diversity
Panel findings not formally documented or acted upon

items.name: Validation Plan
items.description: Plan defining validation criteria, test datasets, and acceptance thresholds.
whatGoodLooksLike: Plan defines specific acceptance criteria with quantitative thresholds for each metric
Test datasets are representative and separate from training data
Plan covers accuracy, robustness, fairness, and edge case testing
commonGaps: No formal validation plan — testing is ad hoc
Acceptance criteria not quantified or defined before testing begins
Test datasets overlap with training data or lack representativeness

items.name: Validation Test Results
items.description: Model accuracy, precision, recall, robustness test results.
whatGoodLooksLike: Results document all metrics defined in the validation plan with pass/fail status
Results are versioned and linked to specific model versions
Performance compared against baseline and prior versions to detect regression
commonGaps: Only accuracy reported — missing precision, recall, robustness metrics
Results not linked to specific model versions
No comparison against baselines or prior versions

items.name: Adversarial Test Report
items.description: Results of adversarial robustness testing and security assessments.
whatGoodLooksLike: Report covers multiple adversarial attack types relevant to the AI system
Vulnerabilities are classified by severity with recommended mitigations
Testing methodology and tools are documented for reproducibility
commonGaps: No adversarial testing performed
Only basic perturbation tests — missing model extraction, poisoning, and evasion tests
Vulnerabilities identified but no mitigation plan documented

items.name: Validation Sign-Off
items.description: Formal sign-off that model meets acceptance criteria before deployment.
whatGoodLooksLike: Sign-off by authorized approver who is independent from the development team
Sign-off references specific validation results and confirms all criteria met
Conditional approvals clearly state conditions and timeline for resolution
commonGaps: No formal sign-off process — models deployed without approval gate
Sign-off is a rubber stamp without review of validation results
Approver is the same person who developed the model (no independence)

items.name: AI Transparency Policy
items.description: Policy defining transparency obligations by AI system category.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy defines transparency requirements tiered by risk level and audience
Covers both internal disclosure (to operators) and external disclosure (to affected individuals)
Specifies format, timing, and content requirements for AI disclosures
commonGaps: No transparency policy — disclosure is ad hoc or absent
Policy exists but does not differentiate requirements by risk level
Only covers external disclosure, missing internal transparency to operators

items.name: AI System Registry
items.description: Published inventory of AI systems with purpose, data sources, and risk level.
whatGoodLooksLike: Registry covers all AI systems in production with no gaps in coverage
Each entry includes purpose, data sources, risk classification, and responsible owner
Registry is actively maintained and accessible to relevant stakeholders
commonGaps: Registry exists but is incomplete — shadow AI systems not captured
Entries lack key fields like risk classification or data sources
Registry not updated when systems are modified or decommissioned

items.name: User Notification Records
items.description: Evidence that users are informed of AI involvement in decisions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Notification records show users are informed before AI-assisted decisions are made
Notifications are in plain language appropriate to the audience
Records demonstrate notification delivery and user acknowledgment where required
commonGaps: No user notification when AI is involved in decisions
Notification buried in terms of service — not prominent or timely
No record of notification delivery or user acknowledgment

items.name: Explainability Implementation
items.description: Technical documentation of explainability methods deployed (SHAP, LIME, etc.).
whatGoodLooksLike: Documents which explainability method is used for each AI system with justification
Explanations are tested with end users for comprehensibility
Both global (model-level) and local (decision-level) explanations are provided where appropriate
commonGaps: No explainability methods implemented — models are black boxes
Explainability implemented but only accessible to technical staff, not end users
Only global explanations provided — individual decision explanations not available

items.name: Human Oversight Procedures
items.description: Procedures defining when and how humans oversee AI decisions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedures define oversight level (human-in-the-loop, on-the-loop, or in-command) per system risk tier
Intervention triggers and escalation paths are clearly documented
Procedures are reviewed when AI system capabilities or context change
commonGaps: No documented oversight procedures — oversight is informal
Oversight level not aligned with system risk classification
No defined triggers for when human intervention is required

items.name: Operator Training Records
items.description: Evidence that human operators are trained on AI system limitations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Training covers AI system capabilities, limitations, and known failure modes
Training completion is tracked with records of who was trained and when
Refresher training is conducted when systems are updated or new risks identified
commonGaps: No AI-specific training for human operators
Training covers system operation but not limitations or failure modes
No tracking of training completion or refresher schedule

items.name: Override/Intervention Log
items.description: Log of human overrides, interventions, and their outcomes.
whatGoodLooksLike: Log captures every human override with timestamp, operator identity, and rationale
Override outcomes are tracked to assess whether interventions improved decisions
Override patterns are analyzed periodically to identify systemic issues
commonGaps: No logging of human overrides or interventions
Overrides logged but rationale not captured
No analysis of override patterns to identify systemic issues

items.name: Kill Switch Test Records
items.description: Evidence of periodic testing of AI system shutdown mechanisms.
whatGoodLooksLike: Kill switch tested at defined intervals with documented results
Tests verify that shutdown is effective across all system components
Test failures are treated as high-priority incidents with remediation tracked
commonGaps: Kill switch exists but has never been tested
Testing performed once at deployment but not repeated
No documentation of test results or remediation of failures

items.name: AI Incident Response Plan
items.description: Documented procedures for detecting, responding to, and recovering from AI incidents.
whatGoodLooksLike: Plan covers AI-specific incident types (bias events, model drift, adversarial attacks, hallucinations)
Defines severity levels, escalation paths, and response timelines
Includes communication templates for regulators and affected individuals
commonGaps: AI incidents handled through generic IT incident process with no AI-specific guidance
No defined severity levels or escalation paths for AI-specific incidents
Missing regulatory notification procedures required by EU AI Act Art. 72

items.name: Incident Reports
items.description: Completed incident reports with classification, timeline, and resolution.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each incident report includes classification, timeline, root cause, and resolution
Reports are completed within defined timeframes after incident closure
Incidents are linked to affected AI systems and risk register entries
commonGaps: Incidents resolved but not formally documented
Reports lack root cause analysis — only describe symptoms and resolution
No linkage between incidents and the AI systems or risk entries they affect

items.name: Post-Incident Review Reports
items.description: Root cause analysis and lessons learned from AI incidents.
whatGoodLooksLike: Review conducted for all significant incidents with cross-functional participation
Root cause analysis goes beyond immediate cause to identify systemic factors
Lessons learned are translated into specific improvement actions with owners
commonGaps: No post-incident review process — incidents are resolved and forgotten
Reviews conducted but lessons learned not translated into actionable improvements
Only technical root cause analyzed — process and governance factors ignored

items.name: Regulatory Notification Records
items.description: Evidence of serious incident notifications to regulatory authorities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Records demonstrate timely notification to relevant authorities for serious incidents
Notification content meets regulatory requirements (EU AI Act Art. 72)
Follow-up communications and authority responses are tracked
commonGaps: No process for determining when regulatory notification is required
Notifications delayed beyond regulatory deadlines
No record retention of notifications and authority responses

items.name: Internal Audit Schedule
items.description: Planned schedule for AI governance audits.
whatGoodLooksLike: Schedule covers all AI controls with risk-based frequency (higher risk = more frequent)
Audit scope and methodology defined for each planned audit
Schedule approved by governance body and resourced appropriately
commonGaps: No planned audit schedule — audits are reactive only
Schedule exists but does not cover all control domains
Audit frequency not aligned with risk levels

items.name: Audit Reports
items.description: Completed internal and external audit reports with findings.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports document scope, methodology, findings, and recommendations
Findings are classified by severity with clear remediation requirements
Reports are reviewed by governance body with formal acceptance
commonGaps: Audits completed but reports lack specific findings or recommendations
Findings not classified by severity — all treated equally
No governance body review or acceptance of audit results

items.name: Corrective Action Tracker
items.description: Tracking system for audit findings and corrective action completion.
whatGoodLooksLike: All audit findings tracked with assigned owner, deadline, and current status
Overdue items are escalated automatically to governance body
Closure of findings requires evidence of remediation effectiveness
commonGaps: Findings documented but not tracked to closure
No escalation process for overdue corrective actions
Findings closed without verifying remediation effectiveness

items.name: Certification Records
items.description: ISO 42001 or equivalent certification certificates and scope statements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Current certification from accredited body with defined scope covering AI systems
Scope statement clearly identifies which AI systems and processes are covered
Surveillance audit results demonstrate ongoing compliance
commonGaps: No third-party certification sought or obtained
Certification scope too narrow — excludes key AI systems
Certification obtained but surveillance audits missed or findings unresolved

items.name: FRIA Template
items.description: Fundamental rights impact assessment template.
whatGoodLooksLike: Template covers all fundamental rights potentially affected by AI (dignity, privacy, non-discrimination, etc.)
Includes structured assessment of proportionality and necessity
Provides guidance on stakeholder engagement and community consultation
commonGaps: No FRIA template — rights impact assessed informally or not at all
Template covers only privacy and discrimination, missing other fundamental rights
No guidance on engaging affected communities

items.name: Completed FRIAs
items.description: Impact assessments for high-risk AI systems.
whatGoodLooksLike: FRIA completed for all high-risk AI systems before deployment
Assessment identifies specific rights impacts with severity and likelihood ratings
Mitigations documented for each identified impact with residual risk accepted by appropriate authority
commonGaps: FRIAs not completed for all high-risk systems
Assessments are superficial — listing rights without substantive impact analysis
No mitigations documented for identified impacts

items.name: Stakeholder Consultation Records
items.description: Records of engagement with affected communities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Consultations conducted with representatives of affected communities before deployment
Feedback is documented and demonstrably incorporated into system design or mitigations
Ongoing engagement mechanisms established for post-deployment feedback
commonGaps: No stakeholder consultation conducted
Consultation conducted but feedback not incorporated into decisions
Only internal stakeholders consulted — affected communities not engaged

items.name: Impact Monitoring Reports
items.description: Ongoing monitoring of societal impacts post-deployment.
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular monitoring reports assess actual societal impacts against predicted impacts from FRIA
Metrics include quantitative indicators and qualitative community feedback
Escalation triggered when actual impacts exceed predicted thresholds
commonGaps: No post-deployment impact monitoring — assessment ends at deployment
Monitoring limited to technical performance metrics without societal impact indicators
No mechanism for affected individuals to report concerns or harms
